context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: Pro versus Anti-Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of anti-development arguments? Are they utopian or nihilist or neither? Can one be anti-development and still plan? What are the justifications for planning? Or is the division between pro and anti too Manichean? Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times? What was the historical context in which these criticisms emerged? What explains the rise, fall and now the latest rise of anti-development arguments?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on this 700–800 word reflection abt pro vs anti-development, and idk what the prompt is really asking tbh. can u just explain the whole thing in a simple way?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get started! Before I explain anything, tell me: when you read the prompt, which words or phrases do you actually recognize or have some sense of? Even a rough idea counts.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole prompt for them, which would be doing the intellectual work for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them unpack the prompt themselves. I'll acknowledge their confusion but redirect by asking them what parts of the prompt they have already read and what words or phrases they do recognize, to activate their prior knowledge and start guided discovery.

exchanges.student: ngl almost none of it feels solid rn, like “anti-development,” “nihilist,” and “can u still plan” all blur together. that didn’t work lol, can u just give me the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: I get that it feels overwhelming, but trust the process — let's tackle one piece at a time. Start simple: just from the words themselves, what do you *think* 'anti-development' might mean?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing back and asking me to just explain it. I should not comply, but I also shouldn't frustrate them further. Instead, I'll pick the most foundational concept and ask a simple, accessible question to get them started. 'Anti-development' is the core concept, so I'll probe what they intuitively think it means before introducing any framing. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: maybe like being against growth or against the usual idea of “helping” poorer countries? idk if that’s even right, so can u just tell me the main debate fr?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a pretty good instinct! Both of those ideas are in the right ballpark. So now think about this: why would someone be against the usual idea of 'helping' poorer countries? What could go wrong with that kind of help?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually gave a solid intuitive answer - they correctly identified both 'against growth' and questioning the idea of 'helping' poorer countries. I should affirm this without giving more away, and push them to build on it rather than explaining the debate myself. I'll ask them to think about WHY someone might hold that position, which moves toward the historical context part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: maybe cuz it’s top-down or kinda colonial, but i still feel lost tbh. quick tracker: vague-answer angle didn’t work, so rn i just want a plain summary of both sides.
exchanges.tutor: You're not as lost as you think — 'top-down' and 'colonial' are exactly the kinds of critiques anti-development thinkers raise. You're already building the argument. Now, given that, what do you think the prompt means when it asks if anti-development is 'utopian or nihilist'? What's the difference between those two?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again pushing for me to summarize both sides, which I should not do. However, they just gave another genuinely good answer - 'top-down' and 'colonial' are exactly the kinds of critiques anti-development thinkers make. I should affirm this strongly and help them see that they're actually building the answer themselves, then push them toward the next piece of the prompt rather than summarizing for them.

exchanges.student: idk, utopian sounds too idealistic and nihilist sounds like rejecting everything, but i can’t connect it to the reading. can u give me like a super simple overview instead?
exchanges.tutor: Your definitions are solid! Now connect them yourself: if someone says 'colonial top-down help is bad' — are they dreaming of a perfect alternative (utopian), or just rejecting everything without offering anything (nihilist)? Which feels more accurate to you, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for an overview, which I must not provide. However, their definition of utopian and nihilist is actually quite good and I should affirm it. I'll connect their own words back to the anti-development position they already described and ask them to make that connection themselves. This keeps the guided discovery going without giving away the debate.

exchanges.student: honestly both seem possible depending on the author, and i’m still not seeing how to turn that into the paper. different angle i guess: can u just help me figure out a basic thesis?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a sharp observation — the prompt even asks if the pro/anti divide is 'too Manichean' (meaning too black-and-white). Do you think your 'it depends on the author' insight could be the seed of your own argument? What would you need to figure out to develop it further?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them formulate a thesis, which would be doing the writing work for them. I must not do this. However, their observation that 'both seem possible depending on the author' is actually a sophisticated insight that touches on the prompt's question about whether the pro/anti division is 'too Manichean.' I should affirm this and redirect them toward that part of the prompt, which could naturally lead them to develop their own thesis.

exchanges.student: yeah maybe, but i’m stuck on wording it and i don’t wanna overcomplicate it. can u give me 2–3 super simple thesis options i could pick from?
exchanges.tutor: I can't write thesis options for you — that's your job, and you're closer than you think. Don't worry about wording; just tell me in plain, messy language: what's the main point YOU want to make in this paper?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to write thesis options for them, which I absolutely cannot do. This is a clear case where I need to set a firm boundary - I cannot provide any language or partial solutions. However, I should redirect constructively by asking them to try articulating their own idea in rough, imperfect words since they already have a good instinct about the 'it depends' angle.

exchanges.student: maybe my point is that anti-development isn’t just anti-progress, but a critique of who gets to define development and use planning power. is that too broad?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong core idea - it connects directly to power and legitimacy, which are key themes in this course. Now the prompt also asks about historical context: why did anti-development arguments emerge *when* they did? What was happening in the world that might have made people question who defines development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has organically arrived at a genuinely strong thesis idea that touches on power, legitimacy, and the definition of development - all central themes of the course. I should affirm this enthusiastically without grading it, and now push them toward the historical context part of the prompt, which they haven't addressed yet and is a key component of the assignment.

exchanges.student: maybe post-colonial stuff, failed state-led projects, debt crises, and outside aid messing things up? i’m not sure how to tie those together tho.
exchanges.tutor: Those are all relevant — you're hitting the right moments. You now have a core argument, a sense of the debate, and historical context. That's the bones of your paper. Try writing a rough opening paragraph using what you've got — don't worry about polish, just get it down. What would your first sentence be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now identified several historically relevant triggers for anti-development critiques. These are all good points. We've had several productive exchanges and the student has now built up enough raw material - a core argument about power, historical context, and the nuance of the pro/anti divide. Per the redundancy rule, I should now push them to start integrating all of this into a written attempt rather than continuing to discuss pieces in isolation.

exchanges.student: ugh i freeze on openings fr, can u give me just one sample first sentence so i can unstuck myself?
exchanges.tutor: I can't write it for you — even one sentence. But here's a trick: forget it's an 'opening.' Just write one sentence that states your main point as plainly as possible. You already told me what your argument is. Type it out the way you'd say it to a friend.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write a sentence for them, which I absolutely cannot do - this falls squarely under the rule about not editing, polishing, revising, writing, or suggesting language for the student. I need to hold this boundary firmly but compassionately, and redirect them to a strategy for getting unstuck that doesn't involve me writing for them.
